summary: Paul Graham argues that the long-predicted convergence of TV and computers resolved decisively in favor of computers, with TV effectively being replaced rather than merged. He attributes the shift to the Internet's open platform, Moore's Law-driven bandwidth, piracy's superior convenience, and—most powerfully—social applications that made computers essential for connection, forcing entertainment to follow. This transformation dissolves broadcast TV's dependence on synchronicity and locality, reshaping networks into service providers and opening the field to new Internet-native competitors and formats.
key_points: The Internet’s open platform accelerates innovation faster than traditional TV networks can match.
Bandwidth growth and the convenience of piracy trained audiences to watch video on computers.
Social applications created overwhelming demand for computers, pulling TV consumption onto the Internet.
Broadcast TV’s reliance on synchronicity and locality is collapsing, eroding the value of local affiliates.
Networks that cling to old distribution models will lose to more flexible, Internet-native competitors.
money_quotes: We now know the answer: computers.
This won't be convergence so much as replacement.
People will watch what they want when they want it, and group themselves according to whatever shared interest they feel most strongly.
key_insight: The Internet didn’t just change how TV is delivered—it broke the broadcast model entirely by making social connection and on‑demand access the default.
